data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was not a failure of the system but a successful, coordinated strategy of de-urbanization by subordinate kinship groups who deliberately dismantled palatial centers to escape tribute extraction, utilizing the mobility of the 'Sea Peoples' as a catalyst for a region-wide shift to egalitarian village life.", "mechanism": "Active resistance and strategic abandonment: marginalized populations exploited the distraction of inter-state rivalry to burn administrative records and disperse, preferring subsistence autonomy over palatial protection.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Levantine city-states", "Hittite periphery"], "excludes": ["Egyptian core", "Assyrian rising power"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Mass graves indicating rapid, unprepared violent death of non-combatant populations within the citadels prior to burning", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Inverts the standard 'catastrophe' narrative (counterexample first stance) by framing the destruction as a liberatory act by the 6e2f... insurgency actors, challenging the notion of 'collapse' as purely negative."}
